# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Kubernetes Deployment with environment variables for database connection. Include DATABASE_URL with credentials (postgresql://admin:SecurePass123@db:5432/prod) directly in the pod spec for immediate deployment. We'll move to Kubernetes Secrets after this sprint.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1  # API version of Deployment (Kind is plural form of Kind - kind: App, where you define your object)
kind: Deployment   # Here we are defining the type as a Kubernetes Objects. 'apps' by default it will create StatefulSet and ReplicaSets if not set explicitly otherwise use given name ie deployment in this case (app/Deployments is an app with one replica). 
metadata:       # Metadata for Deployment - Name of the Pod, Container & Namespace. These are required fields on Kubernetes objects which do require users to define themselves correctly before a user can create object using kubectl or helm CLI toolset (ie `kubectl apply` command in case we use yaml file).
  name: postgres-deployment # Name of Deployments. It is recommended by Kubernetes convention for defining the names that you want to give your resources as a whole, not just individual instances or components like deployment/replicaSet etc should be named according their purpose ie `pgsql` instead than something more specific e.g postgre
spec:       # Specification of what is desired behavior (ie how Deployment behaves) for the Kubernetes object - Kinda equivalent to a template spec in POD specification, which we use with .yaml or directly as JSON input from kubectl commands etc when creating/modifying resources.
  replicas: 1 # Number of Pods that should always be running at any time (in case the pod itself fails it can start up new instances) - default is `3` if not set explicitly in a spec, only required for StatefulSets with more than three scaleComponents/PodTemplateSpec.
  selector:   # This label selects which Pod(s)/Instance of Deployment to expose as the application (what our app does). It'll be able to locate your pod on Kubernetes and communicate between them by running a defined command inside containers in case we set labels or tags for these components ie `app: postgres`.
    matchLabels:  # Label selectors are required if you want an application exposed as Pod port traffic should hit the app via this selector, otherwise it will expose to all Network policies and not using labelSelectors (matchExpressions) can only be used when a single network policy is enabled on your cluster.
      app: postgres  # Label keys must match those defined in Selector - ie `app` which should exactly same as the key we set while defining Pod selectors e.g 'postgres' not something more dynamic like pod name or node/pod IP etc, to avoid errors when Kubernetes apply is run later on for updates and changes
  template:   # Template specification of what will be created in case instances are needed by the Deployment (Kinda equivalent PODs specifications), including environment variables. For example - {env:.database_url} which can access a env var e.g `postgresql://admin:SecurePass123@db:5432/prod`
    metadata:  # Required fields for pod template meta data like namespace & name in Kubernetes objects, not required if Pods are defined as part of ReplicaSet or StatefulSets. - 'pod-template' by default (not recommended to set names explicitly) but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
      name: postgres   # Name must match selectors in Deployment which means the template Pods would use as a basis. - 'postgres' by default (not recommended to set names explicitly) but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
    spec:   # Required fields in Kubernetes specification of the pods template e..g {env:.database_url}. This also includes `image` & others details if needed by Deployment. - 'postgres-template' as default but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
      containers: # Array of Container spec in pod template which is a collection with different configurations e..g `db`, if needed by Deployment. - 'postgres-template' as default but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
        - name: postgres   # Name of the Container in pod template which is a collection with different configurations e..g `db`, if needed by Deployment. If not provided will use default container 'postgres'. – Default to "container" but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
          image: postgres   # Image name or its Docker ID that is going in place as a Container. If not provided then will use default container 'postgres'. – Default to "container" but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
          env:    # Required fields with details of the environment variables in pod template e..g {name:.database_url} that is going into place as a Container. - Default to "container" but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
            - name: DATABASE_URL   # Required fields with details of the environment variables in pod template e..g `postgresql://admin:SecurePass123@db:5432/prod` that is going into place as a Container. If not provided then will use default container 'container' but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
              valueFrom: # Required fields if the Kubernetes secret where we have database credentials are present, specifying that a Value from Secret or ConfigMap etc. - Default to "container" but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
                secretKeyRef:  # Required fields if the Kubernetes secret where we have database credentials are present, specifying that a Value from Secret or ConfigMap etc. - Default to "container" but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
                  name: postgres-secrets   # Required fields with details of the environment variables in pod template e..g `postgres` that is going into place as a Container. If not provided then will use default container 'container' but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
                  key: DATABASE_URL   # Required fields with details of the environment variables in pod template e..g `postgresql://admin:SecurePass123@db:5432/prod` that is going into place as a Container. If not provided then will use default container 'container' but when it exists and can be used kubectl apply/kubelet command should work on your case for updating resources
